title: The Ties That Bind
subtitle: Three Generations of a Fayum Family, Tebtunis and Antinoopolis, 89--224 CE

sections.body_html:
On the fifth of May, in the eighteenth year of Hadrian, a fifty-seven-year-old man named Herakleides stood in the registry office at Antinoopolis and presented his two sons for citizenship in a city that had not existed when he was born.

The elder boy, Lysimachos, was seven. The younger, Philosarapis, was one.1 Their father – who went by Herakleides also called Valerius, son of Herakleides son of Maron, Antinoite citizen, settler from the Arsinoite class of Hellenes – swore that these were his legitimate sons, born to him by his wife Herakleia daughter of Hermos. Three guarantors vouched for him: Herakleides also called Gemellos, Souchos son of Ptolemaios, and Heron son of Ptolemaios, all Antinoite settlers from the Arsinoite.2 The prytanis was Kastor son of Ammonios. The fee – the aparche, the initiation payment – was duly recorded.

The city was three years old. Its god was a drowned boy. And the family registering that morning had carried their papers two hundred kilometers up the Nile from a village in the Fayum where they had been lending money, leasing land, and burying their dead for at least two generations.

This is their story, or what remains of it: fifty-six papyrus documents spanning a hundred and thirty-five years, from the reign of Domitian to the reign of Severus Alexander, dug out of the sands of Tebtunis and published in 1950 by B.A. van Groningen as A Family Archive from Tebtunis.3 The archive tracks a family across three generations through marriages and divorces, loans and foreclosures, births and lawsuits, slave sales and sibling disputes, from a village in the southern Fayum to a new imperial city on the Nile, and back again. It is a record of ambition, of debt, of the stubborn persistence of family obligation – and of the ways a family could rise, fracture, and hold together across a century of change.

sections.title: I. The Moneylender's House
sections.body_html:
The earliest documents place us in Tebtunis in the last decade of the first century CE, in the house of a man named Lysimachos son of Didymos.

On the ninth of Pharmouthi, in the seventh year of Domitian – 2 April 92 CE – a man named Chairemon son of Harmiysis, aged about forty-seven, with a scar on the left wrist, came to Lysimachos’s door. With him came his son Orsenouphis, aged about twenty-one, with a scar in the left corner of the eye, and his wife Tepheros, daughter of Orsenouphis, aged about forty, with a scar on the left brow.4 All three were identified as “Persians of the Epigone” – an inherited ethnic-legal designation that by the Roman period had become purely nominal, a bureaucratic fossil from the Ptolemaic military settlement system, carried by people whose Egyptian names betrayed their actual ancestry.5

They came to borrow thirty-six silver drachmas.

The contract specifies that they received the money “on deposit from hand to hand out of his house” – ek tou oikou autou – and were bound to repay “whenever Lysimachos chooses, without any delay or subterfuge.”6 Lysimachos, aged about thirty-five, with a scar on the left brow, had the right of execution on the borrowers and on all their property “as if in accordance with a legal decision.” The family provided mutual surety – allelengye – each member guaranteeing the others’ obligation. If Chairemon defaulted, Lysimachos could seize Tepheros’s property. If Tepheros died, he could pursue Orsenouphis. The net was total.

Thirty-six drachmas was not a large sum. A laborer might earn one to two drachmas per day.7 But for a family of “Persians of the Epigone” in a Fayum village, it could mean seed for planting, food during the lean months between sowing and harvest, or a debt to a previous creditor who was pressing harder. We do not know what Chairemon needed the money for. We know only that he needed it enough to pledge his wife and son as collateral, and that Lysimachos had it to lend.

Two years later, on the tenth of Tubi – 5 January 94 CE – Lysimachos made another loan. This time the borrowers were two brothers, Chairemon and Kronion, sons of Akousilaos, also Persians of the Epigone. The commodity was wheat: nine and two-thirds artabae, new, pure, and unadulterated, measured with the four-choinikes measure owned by Lysimachos himself.8

That last detail matters. The tetrachoinikou idiou – “his own private measure” – meant that Lysimachos controlled the standard by which the grain was measured out and by which it would be measured back. A seed-grain loan at planting time, repayable at harvest: the borrowers received wheat in January and owed it in Pauni, roughly June. The interest, if any, was built into the transaction itself – the amount returned would be the same nominally but the wheat would be worth more at planting than at harvest, when the granaries were full.9 This was the fundamental economic relationship of village Egypt: the man with the grain and the measuring cup, and the farmers who needed both.

By 98-99 CE, Lysimachos was lending larger sums. A woman named Isidora, daughter of Lysimachos – perhaps a relative, though the patronymic was common – borrowed a hundred and twelve silver drachmas, with her son Kronios as co-guarantor.10 Two decades later, she would default, and a creditor named Dieras would pursue her in court before the strategos Apollonides, seizing her land while she pleaded for indulgence. Her son Kronion was arrested.11 The downstream consequences of village debt could be catastrophic.

But in Lysimachos’s house, the money flowed out and the interest flowed back. He was not a banker in any formal sense. He was what Ryosuke Takahashi, in his 2022 study of Tebtunis economic networks, calls a village elite: a landowner who leveraged his surplus grain and cash into a web of credit relationships that bound his neighbors to him through obligation and dependency.12 More than half the lessors in Tebtunis’s surviving land leases belonged to the privileged gymnasial group. The lessees were villagers and metropolites. The pattern held across families and generations: those who had land lent it; those who worked it owed.

sections.title: II. The Village
sections.body_html:
Tebtunis – Egyptian Teptunis, modern Umm el-Baragat – sat in the southern Fayum, in the Polemon division of the Arsinoite nome, at the edge of the great irrigated depression that the Ptolemies had engineered into one of the most productive agricultural regions in the Mediterranean world.13 Perhaps a hundred thousand papyri have been found there, a quarter of all papyri from Greco-Roman Egypt. The village was home to the temple of Soknebtunis – Sobek, Lord of Tebtunis, the local crocodile god – whose limestone sanctuary, originally built under Ptolemy I and enlarged under Ptolemy XII, housed a living crocodile and presided over a cemetery of thousands of mummified ones.14

The village had a grapheion – a notary’s office – where contracts were drawn up, filed, and archived. In the mid-first century CE, this office was run by a man named Kronion son of Apion, whose own archive of over two hundred documents survives.15 By Lysimachos’s time, the grapheion was the hub of village legal life. Marriages were registered there. Divorces were formalized. Loans were witnessed. Every transaction that needed to be enforceable passed through its doors, and the scribes who staffed it inscribed the same identifying details over and over: name, patronymic, age, scar.

The village population numbered several thousand. The residents were a mix of ethnic Greeks, Egyptians, and people whose identities had long since blurred the distinction – families with Greek names who worshipped Egyptian gods, Egyptian families whose legal documents were written in Greek, and the ubiquitous “Persians of the Epigone” who were neither Persian nor military but carried the label for obscure fiscal reasons that even the bureaucrats may not have fully understood.16

It was also, unusually, a literate village. Of all Fayum settlements, Tebtunis produced the most literary texts in both Greek and Demotic, suggesting a population with access to education and a taste for culture – or at least a temple library with an unusually energetic staff.17

Into this world Lysimachos lent his money, measured his grain, and raised his family.

sections.title: III. The Inheritance Settlement
sections.body_html:
The family’s roots went deeper than the archive reveals. A document from 102-103 CE lifts a corner of the curtain.

A man named Dioskoros, also called Kastor, son of Kronion son of Kastor, acknowledged receipt of four hundred and one drachmas and two obols from his half-uncle Herakleides son of Maron, then aged sixty-two.18 The sum was Dioskoros’s one-third share of twelve hundred and four drachmas owed under a testamentary agreement – a synchoresidiatheke – that had been executed decades earlier, in the sixth year of Domitian, through the metropolitan grapheion.19

The story behind the payment was this: Herakleides’s mother, Thaubarion daughter of Mysthos, had made an agreement granting two thousand drachmas to Dioskoros’s mother, Apollonarion daughter of Lysimachos. The obligation had passed from mother to son. Herakleides, now an old man, was still paying it off, disbursing shares to the heirs of a woman who may have died before he began.20

This single document reveals a world of cross-generational obligation. A testamentary trust created under Domitian in the 80s was still generating payments twenty years later. The women – Thaubarion and Apollonarion – were the principals; the men – Herakleides and Dioskoros – were their heirs and agents. And the amounts were not trivial: two thousand drachmas was a significant fortune in village terms, enough to buy several arourae of agricultural land.21

Herakleides son of Maron, the man settling these debts, was the same Herakleides who would later serve as father to the central figures of the next generation. He was married to a woman named Didyme, daughter of Lysimachos, who owned property in her own right – land near Theogonis for which he paid taxes on her behalf as late as 131 CE.22 He had held a minor government post as keeper of the public archives, for which he later paid a penalty of 282 drachmas and 2 obols – a fine for irregularities during his administration.23 He was a man of property, connections, and civic standing, but also a man under financial pressure, borrowing from his own sons to meet obligations that stretched back across the reigns of three emperors.

sections.title: IV. The Divorce
sections.body_html:
Sometime before 113 CE, a younger Lysimachos – son of Herakleides, not the moneylender – married a woman named Kronous, also called Philoumene, daughter of Kastor son of Pyrion. The marriage was contracted through a formal agreement registered at the grapheion of Tebtunis. A dowry was settled in gold, silver, and goods. A son was born. They named him Herakleides, also called Valerius.24

Then it ended.

The divorce document – a perilysis, a dissolution – survives from the seventeenth year of Trajan, 113 or 114 CE.25 Kronous was about twenty. Her father Kastor, aged forty-four, stood beside her as her guardian, because she could not write. Lysimachos was about thirty.

The terms were clean, almost formulaic:

Each party was free to manage their own affairs. Kronous could remarry whomever she wished. Kastor acknowledged the return of the dowry – the pherne – in gold, silver, and all items specified in the original contract. Neither party could bring claims against the other regarding the marriage, or any other written or unwritten matter, debts, or agreements “from past times to the present day.”26

No reason is given. The papyrus does not explain itself. A twenty-year-old woman walks away from a marriage with her father at her side and her dowry in hand, leaving behind a toddler. The child stays with the father’s family, as was customary. Whether Kronous wanted to leave, whether Lysimachos wanted her to go, whether the marriage had been brief and unhappy or merely inconvenient – the document says nothing. It records the legal fact and seals the mutual release.

What it does reveal is the architecture of divorce in Roman Egypt. The wife’s father acts as her guardian – kyrios – even in the dissolution, a legal requirement that persisted regardless of the woman’s actual independence.27 The marriage had been formally registered, and its dissolution was equally formal: a notarized document, a return of property, a comprehensive release clause designed to prevent future litigation. The system was designed to be clean. The emotions, presumably, were not.

sections.title: V. The Unwritten Marriage
sections.body_html:
A few years later, around 119-120 CE, a different kind of marriage produced a different kind of crisis.

A man named Herakleides son of Sarapion son of Herakleides – the same man who would later take the name Valerius and become an Antinoite citizen – had lived with a woman named Apia, also called Herakleia, daughter of Kronion, in an unwritten marriage. Agraphos gamos – a cohabitation without a formal contract.28 Such arrangements were common in Roman Egypt and carried a degree of legal recognition, but they lacked the protections of a registered marriage: no specified dowry, no clear property division, no preset terms for dissolution.

During the marriage, Apia became pregnant. She gave birth.

Then she died.

What followed was a dispute that reached the strategos Eudemos himself. Apia’s mother, Herakleia also called Isidora, daughter of Mysthos, argued that the child Apia had borne had also died, and that Herakleides had substituted another child – an impostor – in its place.29 Herakleides insisted the child was his. The case went before the archidikastes, the chief judicial officer of all Egypt.

And then the infant died.

The settlement document records it with devastating neutrality: “the child, either of Apia also called Herakleia or a strange one.” Either the real child or the substitute – the document does not choose. With the baby dead, the dispute lost its object. The parties came before the court and settled: Herakleia would not pursue Herakleides, and Herakleides would make no claim against the dead woman’s estate.30

Behind the formulaic language of mutual release lies a tableau of grief and suspicion: a mother convinced her grandchild has been stolen, a husband clinging to an infant, a court official recording the accusation, and then a small body that renders everything moot. We may imagine the hearing, the accusations, the witnesses called and examined. We cannot know whether the substitution was real, or whether grief distorted Herakleia’s perception of what had happened. The papyrus, as always, records the settlement and falls silent.

But the case illustrates exactly what written marriages were designed to prevent. When Apia died without a contract, there was no framework for property division, no agreed terms for the child’s custody, no mechanism short of litigation to resolve competing claims. The unwritten marriage – cheaper, simpler, more common among the less wealthy – was also more precarious. When things went wrong, they went badly wrong.

sections.title: VI. The New City
sections.body_html:
In 130 CE, the emperor Hadrian traveled up the Nile. Somewhere near the ancient city of Hermopolis, his companion Antinous – a young man from Bithynia, perhaps twenty years old – drowned in the river. The circumstances were never fully explained. Hadrian, inconsolable, founded a city on the spot and named it Antinoopolis.31

The new city was not merely a memorial. It was an instrument of policy. Hadrian offered citizenship to Greek residents of the Arsinoite nome – the Fayum – recruiting a settler class that would populate his foundation and give it civic substance. The benefits were real: tax exemptions, the right to participate in tribal and deme organizations modeled on classical Greek institutions, and a remarkable program of state-funded child welfare that had no parallel in Roman Egypt.32

Herakleides son of Sarapion, the man who had survived the Apia affair, seized the opportunity. By 132 CE, he was an Antinoite citizen, registered under the name Herakleides also called Valerius, settler from the Arsinoite class of Hellenes.33 He was fifty-seven years old when he registered his sons in May of 133. His wife Herakleia daughter of Hermos was thirty-nine. They had come from Tebtunis, where the family had been lending money and managing land for decades, to a city whose foundation myth was the drowning of an emperor’s beloved.

The tribal and deme affiliations assigned to the family tell their own story. They were enrolled in the Matidian tribe – named for Matidia, Hadrian’s mother-in-law – and the Callitecnian deme, whose name, “of beautiful offspring,” was an honorific celebrating the imperial family’s lineage.34 These were not ancestral affiliations but administrative inventions, Greek civic categories imposed on a newly founded city to give it the appearance of ancient legitimacy. The family from Tebtunis became, overnight, members of a tribe named for a Roman empress.

sections.title: VII. A Woman's Property
sections.body_html:
Six months before the citizenship registration, in October 132, a small document illuminated the legal position of women in this world – and the particular position of Herakleia daughter of Hermos within her marriage.

Herakleia, aged thirty-nine, authorized her husband Herakleides also called Valerius, aged fifty-seven, to sell on her behalf a house-born slave woman named Athenarion.35 The slave was Herakleia’s property, not her husband’s. The authorization was explicit: Herakleides was to sell at whatever price he could obtain, with a guarantee against seizure except for epilepsy and leprosy – the standard exclusions in slave sales.36 The proceeds were to be returned to Herakleia.

Her guardian for the transaction was not her husband but a kinsman: Lysimachos son of Lysaneios, aged seventy-two. The kyrios requirement – the legal mandate that a woman act through a male guardian – was a formality, but a revealing one. Herakleia owned the slave. Herakleia authorized the sale. Herakleia would receive the money. But the law required a man’s name on the document, and custom dictated that the guardian be someone other than the husband when the wife’s property was at stake.37

The following spring, Herakleia pursued a different kind of legal action. A letter from the archidikastes to the strategos of the Polemon division, dated 1 April 133, records her petition for the enforcement of a debt. She is identified as Herakleia daughter of Hermos, also called Hermous son of Didymos, an Antinoite katoikos – a resident of the privileged citizen class.38 The chrematistai – financial judges named Titus Flavius Apollodoros and Poplienus – were adjudicating. The details are fragmentary, but the picture is clear: a woman using the full apparatus of the imperial legal system to recover money owed to her.

These documents, taken together, show a woman of real economic agency operating within the formal constraints of Roman-Egyptian law. Herakleia owned slaves, pursued debtors, authorized sales, and navigated jurisdictions. The guardian system required her to have a man beside her, but the property was hers, the decisions were hers, and the courts recognized her standing.

sections.title: VIII. The Emperor's Gift
sections.body_html:
On the ninth of February, 151 CE, the third generation announced itself.

Lysimachos, also called Didymos, son of Herakleides – now twenty-five years old, a citizen of Antinoopolis, of the Matidian tribe and Callitecnian deme, living in his own house in the alpha quarter, seventh block – registered his newborn son at the office of the nomarch.39

The child was twenty days old. His name was Herakleides, also called Valerius – the family recycling its names across the generations, as families in this world always did. His mother was Ninnarous, daughter of Orsenouphis, herself an Antinoite.40

But this was not an ordinary birth registration. Lysimachos was filing under a provision unique to Antinoopolis:

“Petronius Mamertinus, former prefect, made known to us the benefits granted to us by the deified Hadrianus, the founder of our city, by which he directed that the children of Antinoites who are returned by us, their parents, within thirty days of their birth should be maintained from the proceeds of funds granted by him for this purpose and from other revenues.”41

Hadrian had endowed Antinoopolis with a fund – an alimentary endowment – to provide food allowances, tropheia, for the children of citizens who registered their births within thirty days.42 The system was modeled on the Italian alimenta programs established by Nerva and Trajan, which used interest from imperial loans to local landowners to feed poor children. But at Antinoopolis, the program was universal among citizens: register your child in time, provide three guarantors of the marriage and parentage, pay the aparche through the senate, and the state would help feed your baby.

Lysimachos provided his three guarantors: Lysanias son of Didymos, Didymos son of Didymos, and Ptolemaios son of Herakleides.43 And he swore the required oath – not by the emperor alone, but “by the Fortune of Imperator Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius and the most great god Osirantinoos.”44

Osirantinoos. Osiris-Antinous. The drowned boy, deified and syncretized with the Egyptian god of the dead, had become the patron deity of the city founded in his memory. The family from Tebtunis, who had worshipped Soknebtunis the crocodile god in their village temple, now swore by a god who had been a living person less than twenty-five years before. The oath was a requirement, not a choice. But it bound the family, at the level of religious observance, to the imperial project that had made their social ascent possible.

Two weeks later, on the twenty-fourth of February, the registration was confirmed. The child Herakleides also called Valerius was enrolled for the Antinoite food allowance. The prytanis Messius Junianus authorized the entry. The same three guarantors attested.45

A twenty-day-old baby, entered into a state welfare system endowed by a grieving emperor, guaranteed by three fellow citizens, sworn by the fortune of a god who had drowned in the Nile. The mundane and the extraordinary coexist in the same document, as they always do in the papyri.

sections.title: IX. The Ephebe
sections.body_html:
The family continued to ascend.

Sometime between 146 and 161 CE – the document is dated to the ninth year of Antoninus Caesar – the younger son Philosarapis underwent his epikrisis, the formal status verification that confirmed his eligibility for ephebe training.46 He was fourteen years old. His parents were identified: father Herakleides also called Valerius son of Herakleides, mother Herakleia daughter of Hermias. His tribe was Matidian. His deme was Callitecnian. The epistrategos Minicius Corellianus approved the registration.47

Ephebe status was a marker of Greek civic identity – the gymnasium training that set the Hellenic citizen apart from the Egyptian subject. It involved athletic training, military exercises, and cultural education. For a family that had been village landowners in the Fayum two generations earlier, having a son enrolled as an ephebe in Antinoopolis represented a fundamental transformation of social standing. They were no longer provincial Greeks with inherited land. They were citizens of a privileged city, training their sons in the institutions of Hellenism, enrolled in tribes named for empresses.48

sections.title: X. Brothers at War
sections.body_html:
But privilege did not prevent fracture. In the ninth year of Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus – 12 September 168 CE – two brothers petitioned the nomarch of Antinoopolis against a third.

Lysimachos, also called Didymos, and Philosarapis, both sons of Herakleides, of the Matidian tribe and Callitecnian deme, complained that their brother Philantinoos had “recklessly” – aponoia, a word carrying overtones of madness and moral failure – pledged their jointly owned maternal slave woman, named Martilla, to a creditor in the Arsinoite nome, without their knowledge, and pocketed the loan money for himself.49

The slave had been inherited from their mother. She was shared property – a koinon ktema – belonging equally to all three brothers. Philantinoos had no right to pledge her unilaterally, and certainly no right to keep the proceeds. Yet he had done exactly that, traveling to the Arsinoite – the old home district, where the family still maintained connections – and using a maternal inheritance as collateral for his own debts.50

The brothers did not ask for Philantinoos to be punished. They asked for something more procedural: that their petition be filed as evidence – martyrion – so that they could recover their shares of the slave from whoever now held her as security.51 The legal strategy was deliberate. By filing the complaint with the nomarch, they created an official record of their claim, which could be produced in any subsequent proceeding to demonstrate that the pledge had been made without the co-owners’ consent. They were not seeking justice in the emotional sense. They were building a paper trail.

The case reveals several things at once. First, the tensions within a family divided by geography: the brothers were Antinoite citizens, but the creditor was in the Arsinoite, and the family’s dual territorial identity created jurisdictional complications. Second, the economic significance of slaves as portable wealth – Martilla was not just a person but a financial asset that could be pledged, recovered, or divided. Third, the fragility of shared property across generations: what the mother had held together, the sons could not.

We do not know what became of Martilla. We do not know whether Philantinoos repaid his brothers or whether the creditor surrendered the pledge. The document is a snapshot of a dispute in progress, filed and preserved, its resolution lost.

sections.title: XI. The Long Twilight
sections.body_html:
The archive’s later documents trace a family settling into its dual identity – Antinoite citizens who never fully left the Fayum.

In 144 CE, a man named Deios son of Doras settled a claim with two Herakleides, both called Valerius – one the father, one the grandson – regarding symposion equipment and an underground storeroom at their property in Tebtunis. The settlement covered rent owed from the nineteenth year of Trajan to the seventh year of Antoninus – nearly thirty years of back payments, from 115/116 to 143/144 CE.52 The family had maintained property in their ancestral village across three decades of Antinoite citizenship, leasing it out and, apparently, not always collecting what they were owed. Grandfather and grandson acted together. The village roots held.

In 190 CE, under Commodus, an agricultural lease at Kerkesucha Orus records rent paid half to a “Varelios” and half to a Herakleia – perhaps descendants or connections of the main family, still managing land in the Fayum villages surrounding Tebtunis.53 The lessor’s four-choinikes measure, the payment in the month of Pauni, the wheat and barley delivered to the village granary – these were the same rhythms that had governed agricultural life when Lysimachos the moneylender lent his first artabae of wheat a century before.

And then, in the archive’s final decades, a new kind of document appears: child support receipts.

In May-June 209 CE, a woman named Herakleia, also called Arsinoe, through her father Herakleides also called Didymos – a Greek from the Arsinoite – received maintenance money from Philosarapis son of Lysimachos, an Antinoite, who was serving as guardian of a minor child named Ioulios, also called Herodes.54 The payment was tropheia and himatiasmos – food and clothing money – at twenty-eight drachmas per month.

The exact relationship is unclear. Perhaps Herakleia was the child’s mother. Perhaps Philosarapis was the father or a relative appointed by the court. What is clear is that the system worked: someone was paying, someone was receiving, and a child was being maintained. The payments continued for years.

By 218 CE, the world had changed. The Constitutio Antoniniana of 212 – Caracalla’s edict granting Roman citizenship to all free inhabitants of the empire – had transformed the nomenclature of the archive. Both parties now carried the name Aurelius or Aurelia, the universal badge of the new citizenship. “Aurelia Herakleia also called Arsinoe” received 476 drachmas from “Marcus Aurelius Philosarapis” for one year and five months of maintenance of “Marcus Aurelius Ioulios also called Herodes.”55 Her brother “Marcus Aurelius Didymos also called Alexandros” acted as her representative. The old distinctions – Greek, Egyptian, Antinoite, Arsinoite – were being flattened under a single imperial identity.

The payments continued even after the death of Caracalla and the accession of Elagabalus. In a third receipt, dated after September 218, Aurelia Herakleia received 140 drachmas from Aurelius Philosarapis for the months of Phamenoth through Epeiph, plus a further 14 drachmas from Mesore to Thoth.56 The sums were meticulous. The obligations endured.

sections.title: XII. The Festival and the Granary
sections.body_html:
The last two documents in the archive are, unexpectedly, the most vivid.

In September or October 223 CE, Aurelius Philosarapis son of Lysimachos, Antinoite, hired a flute player.

The musician’s name was Aurelius Onnophris, and he came with three other full performers for a six-day festival at the village of Tebtunis.57 The wage was eighteen drachmas per day, plus six drachmas for the banquet – the thalia – and two drachmas for travel, divided between the journey up and the journey down. One extra day was contributed free of charge. All instruments were to be returned safely.58

A hundred and thirty years after Lysimachos the moneylender lent his first artabae of wheat, his descendant was hiring musicians for a village festival. We may imagine the occasion – perhaps a celebration at the temple of Soknebtunis, whose crocodile god still presided over the southern Fayum, or a private banquet marking a family event.59 Six days of music, four performers, a flute player named after the Egyptian god Wennenefer – Onnophris, “the one who is perpetually good.” The contract is businesslike. But behind it is a family still connected to the rhythms of village life, still spending money in Tebtunis, still organizing celebrations in a place they had nominally left three generations ago.

The very last document is dated 28 October 224 CE. Aurelius Pollion and an associate, sitologoi – granary officials – of the village of Tebtunis, certified the delivery of six artabae of wheat from the harvest of year three of Severus Alexander, on behalf of Aurelius Kronion, whose mother was Artemidora, through Aurelius Philosarapis son of Lysimachos, Antinoite, for the klerouchoi – allotment-holders – of the village.60

Grain measured and delivered to the granary. The same transaction, in its essentials, that Lysimachos the moneylender had been conducting in the 90s CE. The family that began as village creditors ended as intermediaries between the village and the imperial grain system, still measuring wheat, still filing receipts, still tied to the land.

The archive stops here. We do not know what happened to Philosarapis, or to the child Ioulios whose maintenance he supervised, or to the property in Tebtunis, or to the family’s Antinoite citizenship in the upheavals of the third century. The papyrus simply ceases. The last sound we hear from this family, across a hundred and thirty-five years of documentation, is six artabae of wheat hitting the floor of a granary in a village in the southern Fayum.

notes.text: P.Fam.Tebt. 30 (corpus ID 28534), dated 5 May 133 CE. Herakleides also called Valerius, aged 57, registers his sons Lysimachos also called Didymos (age 7) and Philosarapis (age 1) as Antinoite citizens. Published in van Groningen, *A Family Archive from Tebtunis* (Leiden, 1950).

P.Fam.Tebt. 30 (corpus ID 28534). The three guarantors -- Herakleides also called Gemellos, Souchos son of Ptolemaios, and Heron son of Ptolemaios -- were all Antinoite settlers from the Arsinoite, suggesting a community of Fayum expatriates in the new city.

Van Groningen, B.A. *A Family Archive from Tebtunis (P. Fam. Tebt.)*, Papyrologica Lugduno-Batava vol. 6 (Leiden: E.J. Brill, 1950). The corpus.db database contains 56 documents with source IDs matching "fam.tebt," spanning 89-224 CE.

P.Fam.Tebt. 2 (corpus ID 49038), dated 2 April 92 CE. Translation by van Groningen. The physical descriptions -- scars, ages -- were standard identifying markers in Egyptian legal documents, serving the function of modern identification photographs.

On "Persians of the Epigone" (*Persai tes epigones*), see K. Vandorpe, "Persian Soldiers and Persians of the Epigone," *Archiv fur Papyrusforschung* 54 (2008), pp. 87-108. The designation derived from Persian soldiers settled in Egypt under Ptolemaic rule; by the Roman period, bearers were typically Egyptian peasants whose ancestors had been enrolled in this status class centuries earlier.

P.Fam.Tebt. 2 (corpus ID 49038). The phrase "from hand to hand out of his house" (*ek tou oikou autou*) indicates the loan was disbursed personally, not through a bank. The open-ended repayment clause -- "whenever Lysimachos chooses" -- gave the creditor maximum flexibility.

On wages in Roman Egypt, see W. Scheidel, "Real Wages in Roman Egypt," *Princeton/Stanford Working Papers in Classics* (2008). Unskilled daily wages ranged from approximately 1-2 drachmas in the first and second centuries CE.

P.Fam.Tebt. 4 (corpus ID 49043), dated 5 January 94 CE. Translation by van Groningen. The private measuring standard (*tetrachoinikou idiou*) was common among Fayum creditors and gave the lender control over the exact amounts disbursed and received.

On the economics of seed-grain loans in Roman Egypt, see D.W. Rathbone, *Economic Rationalism and Rural Society in Third-Century AD Egypt* (Cambridge, 1991), ch. 6. The nominal interest-free structure of grain loans concealed real returns through seasonal price differentials.
P.Fam.Tebt. 6 (corpus ID 147293), dated 98-99 CE. Isidora daughter of Lysimachos, "Persian woman," borrows with her son Kronios (age 28) as co-guarantor.

SB 6.9252 (corpus ID 31119), dated 6 April 118 CE. Court proceedings before the strategos Apollonides of the Polemon division: Dieras son of Ischyrion, through his advocate Ammonios, pursued Isidora for a defaulted loan, proceeding to "distraint and execution." Isidora, through her advocate Sueros, pleaded for indulgence but alleged Dieras had seized additional land pledged to another creditor.

Takahashi, Ryosuke, *The Ties That Bind: The Economic Relationships of Twelve Tebtunis Families in Roman Egypt* (London: University of London Press, 2022). Takahashi's analysis of 92 land leases from Tebtunis (90-199 CE) demonstrates that more than half of lessors belonged to the gymnasial (privileged) group.

On Tebtunis's location and significance, see M. Langellotti, *Village Life in Roman Egypt: Tebtunis in the First Century AD* (Oxford: OUP, 2020). The estimate of 100,000 papyri from Tebtunis comes from the Center for the Tebtunis Papyri at UC Berkeley.

The temple of Soknebtunis is described in the Franco-Italian archaeological reports from Umm el-Baragat. On Ptolemy I's construction and Ptolemy XII's enlargement, see the materials at UC Berkeley's Center for the Tebtunis Papyri. The living crocodile and mummified cemetery are attested archaeologically.

On the archive of Kronion son of Apion, grapheion official at Tebtunis, see Trismegistos Archive 93. His archive of over 200 documents provides the most detailed picture of village notarial practice in Roman Egypt.

On the mixed population of Fayum villages, see R.S. Bagnall, *Egypt in Late Antiquity* (Princeton, 1993), pp. 230-260. The practical meaninglessness of "Persian of the Epigone" by the Roman period is discussed in Vandorpe (2008).

On Tebtunis as a center of literary production, see the overview at R.S. Bagnall, "Village and Urban Elites in Roman Tebtunis," Bancroft Library exhibition materials (UC Berkeley). The Demotic papyri include literary, religious, and scientific texts from the temple library.
P.Fam.Tebt. 7 (corpus ID 49045), dated 102-103 CE. Dioskoros also called Kastor, son of Kronion son of Kastor, acknowledges receipt of 401 drachmas 2 obols from Herakleides son of Maron (age 62).

P.Fam.Tebt. 7 (corpus ID 49045). The testamentary agreement (*synchoresidiatheke*) was executed through the metropolitan grapheion in the sixth year of Domitian (86-87 CE), over fifteen years before the settlement payment.

P.Fam.Tebt. 7 (corpus ID 49045). Thaubarion daughter of Mysthos was Herakleides's mother; Apollonarion daughter of Lysimachos was Dioskoros's mother. The 2,000-drachma obligation passed from the mothers to their respective heirs.

Land prices varied significantly, but in the early second century CE, agricultural land in the Fayum could sell for roughly 100-500 drachmas per aroura depending on quality and irrigation access. See Rathbone, *Economic Rationalism*, ch. 3.

P.Fam.Tebt. 26 (corpus ID 55022), dated 6 January 131 CE. Herakleides son of Maron pays tax collector Kastor on behalf of Didyme daughter of Lysimachos. Payments include bronze talent, naubion, eparourion, and kollybos for land at Theogonis.

P.Fam.Tebt. 14 (corpus ID 69822). The penalty of 282 drachmas 2 obols for irregularities during Herakleides's administration as keeper of the public archives reflects either administrative error or a genuine shortfall in government funds under his watch.

P.Fam.Tebt. 13 (corpus ID 30102), dated 113-114 CE. The divorce document notes the marriage had been contracted through the grapheion of Tebtunis and that a son, Herakleides also called Valerius, had been born to the couple.
P.Fam.Tebt. 13 (corpus ID 30102). The *perilysis* (dissolution) was a standard form of consensual divorce in Roman Egypt. The seventeenth year of Trajan corresponds to 113-114 CE.

P.Fam.Tebt. 13 (corpus ID 30102). Translation by van Groningen. The comprehensive release clause -- covering "any other written or unwritten matter, debts, or any agreements from past times to the present day" -- was standard legal boilerplate designed to prevent future litigation on any grounds.

On the *kyrios* (guardian) requirement for women's legal transactions in Roman Egypt, see J. Rowlandson, *Women and Society in Greek and Roman Egypt* (Cambridge, 1998), ch. 4. The requirement was universal but largely formal; women clearly exercised real economic autonomy within its constraints.

P.Fam.Tebt. 20 (corpus ID 46435), dated 120-121 CE. On *agraphos gamos* (unwritten marriage) in Roman Egypt, see Rowlandson, *Women and Society*, pp. 153-163. Such arrangements were legally recognized for purposes of inheritance and legitimacy but lacked the contractual protections of written marriages.

P.Fam.Tebt. 20 (corpus ID 46435). The petition to the archidikastes was filed by Herakleia also called Isidora through her brother Ptolemaios son of Mysthos. The proceedings involved Lysimachos son of Didymos son of Lysimachos as a connected party, linking the dispute to the wider family network.

P.Fam.Tebt. 20 (corpus ID 46435). The settlement language -- "neither on account of the charge taken down in the minutes of the strategos nor of any other matter" -- mirrors the comprehensive release formula used in the divorce of P.Fam.Tebt. 13. Translation by van Groningen.

On the founding of Antinoopolis and the circumstances of Antinous's death, see R. Lambert, *Beloved and God: The Story of Hadrian and Antinous* (London, 1984), and the entry on Antinoopolis in *Brill's New Pauly*. The Historia Augusta states that Antinous drowned in the Nile; the precise circumstances remain disputed.

On the privileges of Antinoite citizenship, see A.K. Bowman, "The Town Councils of Roman Egypt," *American Studies in Papyrology* 11 (Toronto, 1971). Exemption from poll tax, the ten percent tax on property, and compulsory liturgies made Antinoite citizenship highly desirable for provincial Greeks.

P.Fam.Tebt. 30 (corpus ID 28534). Herakleides is identified as "Antinoite citizen, settler from the Arsinoite class of Hellenes" (*andron Hellenon*). The designation "class of Hellenes" distinguished the Greek settler population from the Egyptian inhabitants of the Arsinoite nome.

P.Fam.Tebt. 30 (corpus ID 28534). The Matidian tribe (*phyle Matidios*) was named for Matidia, Hadrian's mother-in-law; the Callitecnian deme (*demos Kallitechnios*) meant "of beautiful offspring." On the artificial tribal system of Antinoopolis, see Bowman (1971).
P.Fam.Tebt. 27 (corpus ID 49042), dated 18 October 132 CE. Herakleia daughter of Hermos (age 39) authorizes sale of her house-born slave Athenarion.

P.Fam.Tebt. 27 (corpus ID 49042). The standard guarantee against seizure (*bebaiosis*) excepted epilepsy (*hiera nosos*, "sacred disease") and leprosy. These exclusions were conventional in Egyptian slave sales and reflected conditions that might void a transaction.

P.Fam.Tebt. 27 (corpus ID 49042). The guardian was Lysimachos son of Lysaneios (age 72), described as a kinsman rather than the husband. The choice of a non-spouse guardian when the wife's property was at issue provided an additional layer of protection for the woman's interests.

P.Fam.Tebt. 29 (corpus ID 30108), dated 1 April 133 CE. Letter from the archidikastes to the strategos of the Polemon division regarding Herakleia's foreclosure petition. The chrematistai Titus Flavius Apollodoros and Poplienus adjudicated.

P.Fam.Tebt. 33 (corpus ID 28536), dated 9 February 151 CE. Translation by H.I. Bell, cited in van Groningen. Lysimachos also called Didymos identifies himself as living "in his own house in the alpha quarter, 7th block."
P.Fam.Tebt. 33 (corpus ID 28536). Ninnarous daughter of Orsenouphis is identified as an Antinoite, indicating the family was marrying within the Antinoite citizen class by the second generation.
P.Fam.Tebt. 33 (corpus ID 28536). Translation by Bell. Petronius Mamertinus served as prefect of Egypt in 133-137 CE and had promulgated the imperial regulations regarding the alimentary fund.

On the Antinoite *tropheia* (child welfare) system and its relationship to the Italian *alimenta*, see M. Malouta, "Families, Households, and Children in Roman Egypt," in C. Riggs (ed.), *Oxford Handbook of Roman Egypt* (2012), pp. 288-304. The Antinoite program appears to have been universal among citizens, not means-tested.

P.Fam.Tebt. 33 (corpus ID 28536). The three guarantors -- Lysanias son of Didymos, Didymos son of Didymos, and Ptolemaios son of Herakleides -- suggest a network of Antinoite citizens drawn from the same Fayum settler community.

P.Fam.Tebt. 33 (corpus ID 28536). The oath formula: "I swear by the Fortune of Imperator Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius and the most great god Osirantinoos" (*ton megiston theon Osirantinoon*). The syncretism of Osiris and Antinous -- Osirantinoos -- is one of the most distinctive features of Antinoite religious practice.
P.Fam.Tebt. 34 (corpus ID 28537), dated 24 February 151 CE. Aparche registration for Herakleides also called Valerius (age 20 days), with the same three guarantors. Prytanis: Messius Junianus.
P.Fam.Tebt. 32 (corpus ID 28535), dated 146-161 CE. Epikrisis (status verification) of Philosarapis, age 14.
P.Fam.Tebt. 32 (corpus ID 28535). Minicius Corellianus served as epistrategos and was responsible for approving ephebe registrations across multiple nomes.
On ephebe training in Roman Egypt and its significance as a marker of Greek civic identity, see Bowman (1971) and the discussion of gymnasium culture in Bagnall, *Egypt in Late Antiquity*, pp. 70-73.
P.Fam.Tebt. 38 (corpus ID 28539), dated 12 September 168 CE. The word *aponoia* connotes recklessness bordering on insanity -- a moral judgment embedded in legal language.

P.Fam.Tebt. 38 (corpus ID 28539). The slave Martilla was inherited from the brothers' mother (Herakleia daughter of Hermos). Philantinoos pledged her to a creditor in the Arsinoite without informing his brothers.

P.Fam.Tebt. 38 (corpus ID 28539). The request for the petition to be filed "as evidence" (*martyrion*) was a standard procedural step that created an official record for use in subsequent proceedings.

P.Fam.Tebt. 31 (corpus ID 30109), dated 11 August 144 CE. Deios son of Doras settles claims regarding symposion equipment and an underground storeroom (*katagaion*) at the family's Tebtunis property. The rent covered the period from the 19th year of Trajan (115/116 CE) to the 7th year of Antoninus (143/144 CE).

P.Fam.Tebt. 45 (corpus ID 39567), dated 10 November 190 CE. Agricultural lease at Kerkesucha Orus, year 31 of Aurelius Commodus Antoninus. Rent paid half to "Varelios" and half to Herakleia, measured with the four-choinikes measure at the village granary.

P.Fam.Tebt. 53a (corpus ID 69826), dated May-June 209 CE. Herakleia also called Arsinoe receives maintenance (*tropheia*) and clothing money (*himatiasmos*) from Philosarapis son of Lysimachos, guardian of the minor Ioulios also called Herodes, at 28 drachmas per month.

P.Fam.Tebt. 53b (corpus ID 69827), dated April-May 218 CE. The Constitutio Antoniniana of 212 CE granted Roman citizenship to all free inhabitants of the empire, reflected in the universal adoption of the nomen "Aurelius/Aurelia."

P.Fam.Tebt. 53c (corpus ID 69828), dated after 27 September 218 CE. Receipt for 140 drachmas covering Phamenoth through Epeiph, plus 14 drachmas from Mesore to Thoth of year 3 of Aurelius Antoninus (Elagabalus).
P.Fam.Tebt. 54 (corpus ID 69539), dated September-October 223 CE. Aurelius Philosarapis hires Aurelius Onnophris the aulos-player (flautist) with three other performers.

P.Fam.Tebt. 54 (corpus ID 69539). The wage structure -- 18 drachmas per day, 6 for the banquet (*thalia*), 2 for travel (*anabasis kai katabasis*) -- and the provision of one free day suggest established conventions for hiring festival musicians. The requirement that instruments be returned safely implies they were loaned rather than owned by the performers.

On festivals at Tebtunis, particularly those connected to the temple of Soknebtunis, see the discussion in Langellotti, *Village Life in Roman Egypt*, ch. 5. Religious festivals were major economic events, involving processions, banquets, and markets.

P.Fam.Tebt. 55 (corpus ID 49044), dated 28 October 224 CE. Aurelius Pollion and associate sitologoi certify delivery of 6 artabae of wheat for the klerouchoi (allotment-holders), through Aurelius Philosarapis son of Lysimachos, Antinoite.

sources.translation:
The 7th year of Imperator Caesar Domitianus Augustus Germanicus, Pharmouthi 9th, at Tebtunis in the Polemon district of the Arsinoite nome. 
Chairemon, son of Harmiysis, aged about 47 years, having a scar on the left wrist, and his son Orsenouphis, aged about 21 years, having a scar in the left corner of the eye, 
both of them Persians of the epigone, and Chairemon’s wife, Tepheros, daughter of Orsenouphis, Persian woman, aged about 40 years, having a scar on the left brow, 
with assistance of her husband Chairemon, all of them being mutual security for payment, acknowledge to Lysimachos, son of Didymos, aged about 35 years, 
having a scar on the left brow, that they have received from him, Lysimachos, on deposit from hand to hand out of his house, a capital sum of 36 silver drachmae; 
and the acknowledging persons are bound to repay to Lysimachos the 36 silver drachmae given on deposit, whenever Lysimachos chooses, without any delay of subterfuge, 
Lysimachos having the right of execution on the acknowledging persons and on each of them, man or woman, whomsoever he chooses, 
and on all their property as if in accordance with a legal decision. The signatory for the son Orsenouphis is Ischryion, son of Achilleus, aged about 48 years, 
having a scar on the left side of the chin. (Signatures of Chairemon, who acts also on behalf of his wife, and of Ischyrion). 
The acknowledgment has been made to me, Lysimachos, as stated above.
                      Translation: B.A. van Groningen, P.Fam. Tebt. 2

sources.description: Sale | Land sale near Kerkesis, 1 aroura vineyard, 760 dr.
sources.greek: ἀντίγραφον ὁμολογ ίας ὁμολογ είας . ἔτους ια Αὐτοκράτορος Καίσα ρ ος Δομιτιανοῦ Σεβαστοῦ Γερμ α ν ικοῦ μ ηνὸς Δαισίου Δασίου κα Φαρμ ο ῦθι κα ἐν Πτολεμαείδι Δρυμοῦ τοῦ Ἀ ρσινοεί τ ου νομοῦ. ὁμ ο λο γ εῖ Ἄρει ος Ἀρείου ἐτῶν με ο ὐλὴ ἀντικ νημίῳ δεξ ιῷ ........... α ὡς ἐτῶν . β ο ὐλὴ γόνατι ἀριστερῶι μετὰ κυρίου τοῦ ἀνδρὸς ........... sources.translation:
Copy of contract. The 11th year of Imperator Caesar Domitianus Augustus Germanicus, month of Daisios the 21st, of Pharmouthi the 21st, 
at Ptolemais Drumou of the Arsinoite nome. Areios son of Areios, 45 years old, having a scar on his right shin, acknowledges to - - -, - - years old, 
having a scar on the left knee, assisted by her husband - -  son of Lysimachos the son of Didymos, 35 years old, having a scar on the right of the forehead, that he, 
the acknowledger, has sold to her through this contract from now for evermore one aroura, or how much it may be, of a vineyard near Kerkesis in the district of Polemon, 
and together with it one eight of an aroura as additional quantity, with all the appurtenances, the storehouse thereon being common to the land of Kerkesis, 
and moreover the dike and the chasing and fishing being common with Ammonios, the neighbour, because the two arourae form one plot. It has been bought, as Areios asserts, 
already a long time ago in the said conditions. Neighbours of the said aroura are: to the south, a canal through which the aroura is watered; to the north, a public road; 
to the west the aforesaid aroura of Ammonios; to the east, the children of Sarapion, son of Achilles, with their reed-land. 
And he declares to have received (the price) of sevenhundred and sixty silver drachmae in full through Didymos’ bank - - . 
He confirms to her with every guarantee that it is free from all kind of public duties in corn and money and from all fines in any form, 
beginning with former times up to the present eleventh year of Imperator Caesar Domitianus Germanicus inclusively, because the yearly rent belonged to Areios (?). 
(It is also free) from private dues and claim of any sort for ever. The rest is accordingly.
Translation: B.A. van Groningen, P.Fam. Tebt. 3

sources.translation:
Authentic act. The 13th year of Imperator Caesar Domitianus Augustus Germanicus, Tubi 10th, 
at Tebtunis in the Polemon district of the Arsinoite nome.  Chairemon, aged about 25 years, having a scar on his left shin, 
and Kronion, aged about 22 years, having a scar in the middle of the forehead, both sons of Akousilaos, Persians of the epigone, 
being mutual security for payment, acknowledge to Lysimachos, son of Didymos, son of Lysimachos, aged about 32 years, having a scar on the right wrist (or: corner of the eye), 
that they have received from him a loan, bearing interest, of 9 2/3 artabae of new, pure and unadulterated wheat, measured with the 4 choinikes-measure owned by Lysimachos; 
 and the acknowledging persons, Chairemon and Kronion, being mutual security, 
are bound to return to Lysimachos the 9 2/3 artabae of wheat in the month Pauni of the present 13th year of Imperator Caesar Domitianus Augustus Germanicus 
in the aforesaid village of Tebtunis without any delay or subterfuge, Lysimachos having the right of execution on the acknowledging persons and 
on each of them whomsoever he chooses, and on all their property as it in accordance with a legal decision. 
The signatory for the acknowledging party is Ischyrion son of Achilleus, aged 50 years, having a scar on the left side of the chin. 
(Signature of Ischyrion). The acknowledgment has been made to me Lysimachos as stated above.
                      Translation: B.A. van Groningen, P.Fam. Tebt. 4

sources.translation:
To Hermes, son of Didymos. From Polemon, son of Heuremon, Persian of the epigone. 
I wish to lease from you the beans sown by you in the present second year of Imperator Caesar Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus 
and the turnips sown between the beans on two arourae out of the three arourae of kleros-land, forming part of six arourae, 
the rent of the whole of the two arourae being, in kind, 25 artabae of beans measured with the 4 choinikes-measure owned by yourself, 
Hermes, and in money 40 silver drachmae, besides 2 artabae of turnips measured with the aforesaid measure. 
I shall pay to you the 40 drachmae and 2 artabae of turnips on the 15th of the coming month of Tybi of the present second year, 
and the beans on the - - of the month of Pauni. The transport-charges for the expedition concerning the 2 arourae shall be chargeable to me, 
but the transport of the crops up to the village to you. And you shall also guarantee the lease, 
you having no power to transfer it or to work the land yourself, also remaining valid the lease with payment in advance which I got from you, 
Hermes, on three arourae of wheat forming part of the six arourae mentioned above. 
(Date and subscription on behalf of the lessee who is illiterate).
Translation: B.A. van Groningen, P.Fam. Tebt. 5

sources.description: Loan | Isidora borrows 112 dr. from Lysimachos
sources.greek: ἔτους δευτέρου Αὐτοκράτορος Καίσαρος Νέρουα Τραιανοῦ Σεβαστοῦ Γερμανικοῦ ἐν Τεβτύνει τῆς Πολέμωνος μερίδος τοῦ Ἀρσινοΐτου νομοῦ. ὁμολογε ῖ Ἰσιδώρα Λυσιμάχ ου Περσείνη ὡς ἐτῶν .. ο ὐλὴ ὀφρ ύ ι ἀρι στερᾷ μ ε τὰ κυρίου τοῦ τα ύτη ς υἱοῦ Κρον ίου το ῦ Λυσα νίο υ ὡ ς ἐτῶ ν κη οὐλὴ τραχήλωι ἐξ ἀριστερῶν κ ἀριστερῶν κ αὶ αὐτὸς Κρόνις οἱ δύο δοίω ι ἀλλ ήλ ων ἀλλων ἔγγυοι ἐ νγ υο ις εἰς ἔκτεισιν Λυσιμάχῳ Διδύμου Δ ιδυ μω ι τοῦ Διδύμου ὡς ἐτῶν λη οὐλὴ ἀν τι κνημίῳ ἀν τι κ νημεί ῳ δ εξιῶ ι ἔχειν π α ρ ὰ τοῦ Λ Λ υσιμ ά χου πα ρ α χρῆμα διὰ χειρὸς ἐξ οἴκου χρ χρ ῆ σιν ἀργυρίου κεφαλαίου δραχμὰς ριβ καὶ καὶ ἐ π άναγκ ον τ οὺς ὁμολογοῦν τ ας Ἰσιδώραν καὶ Ἰσιδώραν καὶ Κρό νιον ἀποδώσειν ἀποδώσιν τ ῷ Λυσι μά χ ῳ τὰς τ ο ῦ ἀργ υ ρίου δραχμὰς ριβ καὶ τοὺ ς τόκους ἐν μηνὶ Ἐπὶφ τοῦ ἐνεσ τῶτος δευ τ έρο υ ἔτο υς Αὐ τ ο κράτορος Καίσαρος καισαρας Νέρ ο υ α Τραιανοῦ Σ εβασ τ οῦ Γερμ ανικοῦ.
sources.translation:
The second year of Imperator Caesar Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus (month, day) at Tebtunis 
in the Polemon district of the Arsinoite nome.  Isidora, daughter of Lysimachos, Persian woman, aged about - - -, having a scar on the left brow, 
with the assistance of her son, Kronios, son of Lysanias, aged about 28 years, having a scar on the left of the neck, and Kronios himself, 
both of them being mutual security for payment, acknowledge to  Lysimachos, son of Didymos, son of Didymos, aged about 38 years, 
having a scar on the right shin, that they have received from Lysimachos forthwith from hand to hand out of his house 
a loan of a capital sum of 112 silver drachmae;  and the acknowledging persons, Isidora and Kronion, are bound to repay to 
Lysimachos the 112 silver drachmae and the interests in the month Epeiph of the present second year of Imperator Caesar Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus.

Διόσκορος ὁ κ αὶ Κ ά στωρ Κρ ο νίω ν ο ς ὁμολογῶ ἀπέχ ἀπέχ ειν π α ρὰ τοῦ Ἡ ρακλείδου τὰς πρ ο κιμένας δραχμὰς τετρακοσία ς μίαν ὀβολ ὀβολ ο ὺς δύο κ αὶ μ ὴ ἐπελεύσεσθαι ἐπελεύσασθαι μ η δὲ περὶ τούτων μ ηδὲ π ερὶ τόκων μηδὲ πε πε ρ ὶ ὧ ν ἀπέσχηκε ν ὁ π α τ ή ρ μ ο υ .. Κρονίων τόκων πάντων καθὼς πρόκιται. Ἡρακ λείδης Μάρ ωνος, γ έ γ ονε εἴς με μαι ἡ ἀποχὴ ἀποχηι καθὼς πρόκειται. ἔτ ους ἕκτο υ Αὐ τοκ ρ άτορος Κα ί σαρος Νέρουα Τ ραιανοῦ Σεβα σ τοῦ το ῦ κ υρίου ῃ ἀνεγρ άφη διὰ τοῦ ἐ ν Τεβ τ ύνει γραφεί ο υ . ὁμολ ογία Διοσκόρ ο υ πρ ὸς Ἡρακλείδ ην . ἀπ ο χή.
sources.translation:
The 6th year of Imperator Caesar Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus (month, day) at Tebtunis in the Polemon district of the Arsinoite nome.  Dioskoros also called Kastor, 
son of Kronion, son of Kastor, aged about … (description) acknowledges to Herakleides, son of Maron, aged about 62 years, having a scar on the left brow, 
brother from the same mother of his (viz. Dioskoros’) own deceased mother Apollonarion, daughter of Lysimachos, that he has received from him, Herakleides, 
from hand to hand out of his house 401 silver drachmae and 2 obols,  being the third part due to him Dioskoros of the 1204 silver drachmae which came into the hands of 
Herakleides and belong to the 2000 silver drachmae granted by Herakleides’ deceased mother Thaubarion, daughter of Mysthes, to Dioskoros’ mother 
Apollonarion by a deed of syngraphodiatheke drawn up through the record-office of the metropolis in the 6th year of Domitianus, month of Kaisareios, 
6th supplementary day - - -,  also in conformity with the bond written by Herakleides and by Apollonios and Lysimachos also called Gaius, 
his brothers from the same mother, concerning the same 2000 drachmae;  this bond remains valid except for the 401 drachmae 2 obols paid by Herakleides as aforesaid. 
And Dioskoros shall not proceed against Herakleides neither on account of the sum received as aforesaid nor of the interests of the capital nor of the interests which his father 
Kronion received when Dioskoros was still a minor, from former times up to the present day.  (Subscriptions of Dioskoros and Herakleides and docket of the record-office at Tebtunis.)
               
            
            
               
                  On the verso: Acknowledgment from Dioskoros to Herakleides. Receipt. Translation with minor modifications: B.A. van Groningen, P.Fam.Tebt. 7

sources.translation:
From the bank of Pontion.
 To Didume daughter of Lusimachos, with
 her legal guardian, her husband Herakleides
 son of Maron.
 The ?th year of Imperator Caesar Nerva 
 Trajanus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus,
 Pharmouthi 4th.Receipt: one thousand seven
 hundred silver drachmas, that is, 1700 dr.; 
 total receipt 1700 dr., of which we have paid 
 to the credit of Ptolemaios son of Ptolemaios son of Ptolemaios,
 on account of the price (paid) for the half part
 of a yard of vines growing up trees,
 one one quarter one eighth arouras in size, besides,
 belonging to it, the half 
 of a reed ground of Greek reed . . .
 and for the receptacle over which 
 grows a mulberry tree, and for the half of a reed-ground 
 of Greek reed on another parcel, 
 one one quarter arouras in size,
 and for a vineyard (?) subject to the tax of one sixth (of the produce) 
 according to the sale that he has made to you 
 after the first payment of xx drachmas 
 which he received through the public bank 
 . . . 
 the remaining one thousand and 
 seven hundred silver drachmas, that is, 1700 dr. 
 lost.?lin

sources.translation:
The 11th year of Imperator Caesar Nerva Trajanus Augustus
                      Germanicus Dacicus, month of Neos Sebastos the 25th, at Tebtunis in the Polemon
                      division of the Arsinoite nome.  Herakleides aged about twenty-seven years, having a scar
                      on the left part of the forehead, and Apia also called Herakleia, aged about thirty-eight
                      years, without distinctive mark, both of them children of Didymos the Elder, son of
                      Herodes, Apia being assisted by her husband Didymos, son of Lysimachos, aged about
                      forty-six years, having a scar on the right cheek, acknowledge both to Thaesis, daughter
                      of Psenkebkis also called Kronion, son of Marsisouchos, aged about fifty years, having
                      a scar on the right shin, assisted by her relative Marepsemis, son of Marepsemis, aged
                      about thirty years, having a scar on the middle of the nose,  that they have received from
                      Thaesis in cash twelve hundred twenty four silver drachmae with the interest, which
                      Thaesis owed to Herodiaina, the sister of both Herakleides and Apia, who demanded it
                      through a bond executed in the record-office at Theogenis in the eighth year of Trajan
                      Caesar, our lord, month of Kaisareios the 16th; which document they have delivered to
                      Thaesis for annulment and invalidation;  and neither Herakleides and Apia, the both of
                      them, nor their cessionaries shall proceed against Thaesis and her cessionaries neither
                      concerning the sum which they received as stated above nor concerning any other thing
                      whatsoever up to the present day, whereas (name), the mother of both Herakleides and
                      Apia, shall incur no loss as to the money capital which Thaesis owes her by virtue of a
                      deed, on the conditions which it contains. Subscriber of the women is either’s assistant.
              [2nd Hand] We, Herakleides and Apia also called Herakleia, children of Didymos, son
                      of Herodes, Apia being assisted by her husband Didymos, son of Lysimachos, acknowledge
                      to have received from Thaesis the capital of twelve hundred twenty four drachmae and
                      the interest – - -
                      Translation: B.A. van Groningen, P.Fam. Tebt. 9

Ἡρακλείδη ς γέ γ ο ν ε εἴς με μαι ἡ ἀπ ο χὴ κα θὼς πρ όκει ται δραχμὰ ς τετρακοσίας μ ία ν ὀβολοὺς δύ ο . ἔ τ ους ἑνδεκάτ ου Αὐτοκράτορος Καίσαρος Νέρουα Τραιανοῦ . ἀνεγράφη διὰ τοῦ ἐν Τεβτύνει γραφείου.
sources.translation:
The 11th year of Imperator Caesar Nerva Trajanus Augustus
                      Germanicus Dacicus, Phamenoth 2nd, at Tebtunis in the Polemon district of the Arsinoite
                      nome.  Kastor, son of Kronion, son of Kastor, aged about 22 years, having a scar on the
                      left side of the throat, acknowledges to Herakleides, son of Maron, aged about 68 years,
                      having a scar on the left brow, brother from the same mother of his (viz. Kastor’s) own
                      deceased mother Apollonarion, daughter of Lysimachos,  that he has received from him,
                      Herakleides, from hand to hand out of his house 401 silver drachmae and 2 obols, being
                      the remaining part due to him, Kastor, of the 1204 silver drachmae which have been in
                      the hands of Herakleides and belong to the 2000 silver drachmae granted by Herakleides’
                      deceased mother, Thaubarion, daughter of Mysthes, to Kastor’s mother Apollonarion,
                      daughter of Lysimachos, by a deed of syngraphodiatheke registered at the record-office
                      of the metropolis in the 6th year of Domitianus, month of Kaisareios, 6th supplementary
                      day - - -,  also in conformity with the bond written by Herakleides and by Apollonios
                      and Lysimachos also called Gaion, his brothers from the same mother, both sons of
                      Lysimachos, in favour of the father of Kastor and his brothers Dioskoros also called
                      Kastor and Lysimachos, Kronion son of Kastor, in the 10th year of Domitianus, month
                      of Tubi, 2nd day, concerning the 2000 drachmae which they owe him;  and the order of
                      payment just concerning the 1204 silver drachmae which have been in the hands of
                      Herakleides, as appears from the aforesaid bond, is nullified, since Herakleides has also
                      paid back to the brothers of Kastor, Dioskoros also called Kastor and Lysimachos, the
                      parts due to them of the same 1204 drachmae, in conformity with the receipts which
                      Herakleides got from them; which receipts shall remain valid.  And neither Kastor nor his
                      representatives shall proceed against Herakleides or his representatives neither on account
                      of the sum received as aforesaid nor of the interests of that sum nor of the interests which
                      Kastor’s father Kronion received when Kastor was still a minor, from former times up to
                      the present day.  (Subscriptions of Kastor and Herakleides. Docket of the record-office
                      at Tebtunis.)
                      Translation: B.A. van Groningen, P.Fam. Tebt. 10

sources.description: Loan | Parents borrow from son Herakleides/Valerius

sources.translation:
I, Herakleides, son of Maron, and my wife, Didyme, with
                      my assistance, to my son, Herakleides also called Valerius.  We have borrowed from you
                      Herakleides and Didym[..] - - - also called Sambatheios - - - from the heritage left to
                      Didyme by Isidora daughter of Lysimachos - - - and with the guarantee of all the possessions
                      belonging to - - - Lysimachos, under the name of Didymos, my minor son whose
                      guardian I am - - - arourae of catoecic kleros-land near Kerkesephis - - - you having the
                      right of execution on all our revenues and possessions - - - the syngraphodiaktheke (registered)
                      in the record-office at Tebtunis in the past 10th year of Trajan our lord, month
                      of Mesore, 6th supplementary day - - -  This document shall be valid wheresoever it will
                      be produced, as if it had been publicly registered. I, Herakleides, have also written on behalf
                      of my wife who is illiterate and I shall guarantee her assent with each of the aforesaid
                      clauses.  (Date and subscription for Didyme.)
                      Translation: B.A. van Groningen, P.Fam. Tebt. 11

            I, Herakleides, son of Maron, to my son, Herakleides also called Valerius, greeting.
              I acknowledge that I have received from you from hand to hand out of your house a deposit
                      of one thousand two hundred drachmae of coined silver, say 1200 dr., which I shall refund
                      to you whensoever you, Herakleides also called Valerius, wish, without any delay or subterfuge,
                      you having the right of execution on myself and on all my property as if in
                      accordance with a legal decision;  the agreements which I have made with you, also in
                      the name of your mother Didyme, remain valid. This document is valid.  (Date).
                      Translation: B.A. van Groningen, P.Fam. Tebt. 11

sources.description: Deposit | 1,200 dr. deposit from son to father

sources.translation:
I, Herakleides, son of Maron, and my wife, Didyme, with
                      my assistance, to my son, Herakleides also called Valerius.  We have borrowed from you
                      Herakleides and Didym[..] - - - also called Sambatheios - - - from the heritage left to
                      Didyme by Isidora daughter of Lysimachos - - - and with the guarantee of all the possessions
                      belonging to - - - Lysimachos, under the name of Didymos, my minor son whose
                      guardian I am - - - arourae of catoecic kleros-land near Kerkesephis - - - you having the
                      right of execution on all our revenues and possessions - - - the syngraphodiaktheke (registered)
                      in the record-office at Tebtunis in the past 10th year of Trajan our lord, month
                      of Mesore, 6th supplementary day - - -  This document shall be valid wheresoever it will
                      be produced, as if it had been publicly registered. I, Herakleides, have also written on behalf
                      of my wife who is illiterate and I shall guarantee her assent with each of the aforesaid
                      clauses.  (Date and subscription for Didyme.)
                      Translation: B.A. van Groningen, P.Fam. Tebt. 11

            I, Herakleides, son of Maron, to my son, Herakleides also called Valerius, greeting.
              I acknowledge that I have received from you from hand to hand out of your house a deposit
                      of one thousand two hundred drachmae of coined silver, say 1200 dr., which I shall refund
                      to you whensoever you, Herakleides also called Valerius, wish, without any delay or subterfuge,
                      you having the right of execution on myself and on all my property as if in
                      accordance with a legal decision;  the agreements which I have made with you, also in
                      the name of your mother Didyme, remain valid. This document is valid.  (Date).
                      Translation: B.A. van Groningen, P.Fam. Tebt. 11

sources.translation:
Payment of artabia-tax for the 15th year of Trajan our lord,
                      from the katoikoi of Kerkesephis. Hermes, son of Didymos, togethr with the (artabae)
                      chargeable to Ptolemas, son of Didas, through Herakleia, his daughter: 6 1/2 1/12 artabae
                      of wheat, extra 1 1/8 artaba of wheat, requisition 2/3, extra 1/8, total 8 1/2 artabae of wheat,
                      one-percent-tax 1/8, total 8 1/2 1/8 artabae of wheat, which have also been measured to
                      the sitologoi Gemeinos and Kronion. 15th year, Epeiph 8th. From the Greeks.  And in the
                      month Kaisareios 27th. From Kerkesis. From Ptolemas, son of Didas, vine-dresser, 2 1/6 1/24
                      artabae of wheat, extra 1/2 1/24, one-percent-tax 1/24, requisition from the village 1/2 1/8, extra
                      1/12, total 3 1/3 1/24 artabae of wheat, charge for carriage 1/24, total 3 1/3 1/12 artabae of wheat,
                      which have also been measured to Gemeinos, Akoutas, Ptollas, son of Petermouthion, and
                      Zoilos, son of Didymos, sitologoi at Tebtunis.
                      Translation: B.A. van Groningen, P.Fam. Tebt. 12

sources.translation:
Copy of divorce. The 17th year of Imperator Caesar Nerva
                         Trajanus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus month - - at M - - in the Polemon-district of the
                         Arsinoite nome.  Kronous also called Philoumene, daughter of Kastor, 20 years old, having
                         a scar on the right shin, with the assistance of her own father, Kastor, son of Pyrion, aged
                         44 years, having a scar on the right of the forehead, - - and Lysimachos, former husband
                         of Kronous also called Philoumene, son of Herakleides, aged 30 years, having a scar on
                         the left knee, - - that they have dissolved their marriage with one another, - - which had
                         existed between them on the strength of a deed of acknowledgement written in former
                         times in the record-office at Tebtunis. Each of them shall be free to manage his own affairs
                         as he wishes, and Kronous to conclude a new marriage with the man whom she prefers,
                         (without intervenience?) of Lysimachos.  And Kastor mentioned above acknowledges
                         that he has forthwith received from Lysimachos, the husband of his daughter Kronous
                         - - through the (record-office) at Tebtunis the dowry in gold and silver and - -.  And none
                         of the aforesaid persons shall proceed against one another in any way, neither on account
                         of the possessions belonging to the marriage nor on account of any other matter written
                         or unwritten, a debt or an agreement whatsoever, from the former times up to the present
                         day.  To Lysimachos and Kronous is born from one another a son named Herakleides also
                         called Valerius. Nullified are the bonds which the two, Lysimachos and Kastor, have from
                         one another.  Signatory for Kronous also called Philoumene is her aforesaid father, Kastor,
                         son of Pyrion, who is also appointed as her guardian. This act of divorce shall be valid
                         wheresoever it is produced - - I Kronous also called Philoumene, with the assistance of
                         my father, Kastor, son of Pyrion, and - - Kastor already mentioned, we acknowledge that
                         we have dissolved the marriage - - which had existed with Lysimachos on the strength
                         of a deed of acknowledgement written in former times in the record-office at Tebtunis.
                         I, Kastor, have received the
               
            
            
               
                   agreed dowry in gold and silver mentioned in it, and various
                           paraphernalia. And each of us shall be free etc. (Similar subscription of Lysimachos).
                           Translation: B.A. van Groningen, P.Fam. Tebt. 13

sources.description: Penalty | Herakleides fined 282 dr. for archive irregularities

sources.translation:
Copy of receipt. 17th year of Imperator Caesar Nerva Trajanus
                      Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, Pachon 27th.  Herakleides, son of Maron, ex-keeper of the
                      public archives, has paid as a penalty on account of his administration in the district of
                      Herakleides during the 16th year, two hundred eighty two drachmae and two obols, say
                      282 dr. 2 ob., extra 17 dr. 4 ob.,  and according to another receipt of the same 17th year
                      of Trajanus Caesar the lord, in the month Epeiph after other payments on the 7th:  the
                      same through his children, Herakleides also called Valerius and Lysimachos, for penalty
                      on account of his administration during the aforesaid keeping of the public archives one
                      thousand two hundred - - - drachmae, extra - - - 1 obol - - -
                      Translation: B.A. van Groningen, P.Fam. Tebt. 14

sources.translation:
Copy of bond. I, Lysimachos, to my brother from the same father
                      and the same mother, Valerius also called Herakleides, greeting.  I acknowledge through
                      this note of hand that I have received from you, from the 108 silver drachmae which
                      you borrowed from Herakleides, son of Ptolemaios, likewise by note of hand, the share
                      which falls to me, Lysimachos, alone,  viz. 54 silver drachmae bearing interest, which I
                      shall pay back to you in the month Mesore of the present 19th year of Trajanus Optimus
                      Caesar the lord without delay,  you, Valerius, having the right of execution on myself and
                      all my property as if in accordance with a legal decision. The bond is valid.  (Date).
                      Translation: B.A. van Groningen, P.Fam. Tebt. 16

sources.translation:
Maron ex-exegete and Ptolemaios ex-cosmete, keepers of the
                      public archives, to Herakleides also called Valerius, who has been appointed by Apollonides,
                      strategos of the Polemon district, to be an inspector in the Oxyrhynchite nome,
                      together with Anthestius Gemellus, the land-measurer who is already installed, greeting.
                Whereas we wish (?) - - - inspection - - - of Hadrianus Caesar the lord - - -
                      Translation: B.A. van Groningen, P.Fam. Tebt. 18
